title: Subject-Verb Agreement
definition: The verb in a sentence must agree with its subject in number (singular/plural) and person.
formula: Singular Subject + Singular Verb  |  Plural Subject + Plural Verb
rules: A singular subject takes a singular verb: He runs. The dog barks.
A plural subject takes a plural verb: They run. The dogs bark.
Two subjects joined by 'and' usually take a plural verb: Ravi and Priya are friends.
Subjects joined by 'or/nor' agree with the nearer subject: Neither the teacher nor the students were late.
Collective nouns (team, family, group) usually take a singular verb when acting as one unit: The team is playing well.
Indefinite pronouns like 'everyone', 'everybody', 'somebody', 'nobody' take a singular verb: Everyone is happy.
Words between subject and verb don't change agreement: The box of chocolates is on the table (subject is 'box', not 'chocolates').
examples: She plays the piano every evening.
The children play in the park.
Ravi and his friends are going to the movies.
Neither he nor his brothers were present.
Everyone in the class has submitted the assignment.
The list of items is on the desk.

commonMistakes.wrong: The list of items are on the table.
commonMistakes.right: The list of items is on the table.
commonMistakes.why: The subject is 'the list' (singular), not 'items'; ignore the prepositional phrase in between.
Indefinite pronouns like 'everyone' take a singular verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: Neither of the boys were late.
commonMistakes.right: Neither of the boys was late.
commonMistakes.why: 'Neither' is treated as singular in formal English.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: Collective nouns like 'team' usually take a singular verb.

quiz.question: The box of chocolates ___ (be) on the table. (present)

quiz.sentence: The number of students are increasing.
quiz.correction: The number of students is increasing.
quiz.explanation: 'The number of' takes a singular verb, unlike 'a number of' which is plural.
